text: The patient is placed in a standard right lateral decubitus position.
An H-shaped skin incision is performed on the fourth intercostal space.
Subcutaneous tissues are dissected to reach the muscular layer and latissimus dorsi and pectoralis major are identified and spared.
The furred rib is then isolated and cut.
The parietal pleura appears thick and is digitally opened.
The pleural cavity is full of purulent material and the bronchopleural fistula is clearly visible.
The fourth and the second ribs are then isolated and removed to enlarge the thoracostomy.
Plural cavity is then deeply cleaned and abrided and the purulent material is removed.
Washings with saline solution and betadine are performed.
Finally, the skin flaps are sutured directly to the parietal pleura with interrupted absorbable suture, anchored to the rib stems to epithelialize the borders and maintain the patency of the window.
Skin is sutured to obliterate the margins and sharp the wound.
Gauzes are soaked with betadine and plugged in the pleural cavity after that the bronchial fistula has been protected with a sponge.
The cavity is filled with gauzes and the wound medicated.
In the post-operative time, during the hospital stay, the patient underwent daily dressing changes to ensure the sterilization of the cavity and eradicate local sepsis.
